questions.question: Consider a B+ tree in which the search key is 12 bytes long, block size is 1024 bytes, record pointer is 10 bytes long and the block pointer is 8 bytes long. The maximum number of keys that can be accommodated in each non-leaf node of the tree is ______.
questions.to_find: Maximum number of keys in each non-leaf node of the B+ tree

understand.plain: A B+ tree non-leaf (internal) node stores n block pointers and (n-1) search keys. We need to fit as many entries as possible within one disk block. By setting up the size inequality and solving, we find the maximum order n, and the number of keys is n-1. Note that non-leaf nodes use only block pointers (not record pointers).

keywords.explain: An internal node in a B+ tree that stores only keys and child (block) pointers; it does NOT store record pointers.
keywords.example: A non-leaf with order 4 stores 4 block pointers and 3 keys.

keywords.explain: The maximum number of child pointers a non-leaf node can hold; a node with order p has at most p-1 keys.
keywords.example: Order 3 means at most 3 children and 2 keys per internal node.

keywords.explain: All pointers and keys in a node must fit within one disk block; this sets the maximum order.
keywords.example: With block size 1024 bytes, we solve p*8 + (p-1)*12 <= 1024 to find p.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 90' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='system-ui,sans-serif' font-size='12'><rect x='10' y='20' width='400' height='50' rx='6' fill='var(--color-bg-secondary,#F0F0EE)' stroke='var(--color-border,#BDBDBA)'/><text x='20' y='42' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'>Non-leaf: [P1 | K1 | P2 | K2 | ... | K(p-1) | Pp]</text><text x='20' y='62' fill='var(--color-text-secondary,#6B6B68)'>p block pointers x 8 bytes + (p-1) keys x 12 bytes <= 1024</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: B+ tree non-leaf node: p block pointers and p-1 search keys must fit within 1024 bytes.

given.aim: Find the maximum number of keys in a B+ tree non-leaf node given storage parameters.

terms.meaning: 12 bytes per key in the index
terms.example: A 12-byte key might store a student ID string.
terms.connects: Each key in the non-leaf node consumes 12 bytes.
A non-leaf node with p pointers uses p * 8 bytes for block pointers.

terms.meaning: 10 bytes per record pointer (used only in leaf nodes of B+ tree)
terms.example: Leaf nodes use record pointers to point to actual data tuples.
terms.connects: Not used in non-leaf node calculation; only block pointers appear in internal nodes.

given.plan: For a non-leaf node with order p: it contains p block pointers and (p-1) keys. Set up p * 8 + (p-1) * 12 <= 1024, solve for the maximum integer p, then keys = p - 1.

steps.title: Set up the size inequality for a non-leaf node
steps.note: We take the floor: p_max = 51. Note: record pointer (10 bytes) is not used in non-leaf nodes of B+ trees.

steps.title: Compute the number of keys
steps.note: A non-leaf node of order 51 stores 51 block pointers and 50 search keys.

questions.formula_note: B+ tree non-leaf node capacity: p * block_pointer_size + (p-1) * key_size <= block_size; maximum keys = p - 1.

questions.question:
B+ Trees are considered BALANCED because
A. The lengths of the paths from the root to all leaf nodes are all equal.
B. The lengths of the paths from the root to all leaf nodes differ from each other by at most 1.
C. The number of children of any two non-leaf sibling nodes differ by at most 1.
D. The number of records in any two leaf nodes differ by at most 1.
questions.answer: A. The lengths of the paths from the root to all leaf nodes are all equal.
questions.to_find: The correct reason why B+ trees are considered balanced

understand.plain: A balanced tree is one where all paths from the root to the leaves are the same length. B+ trees guarantee this property because every insert and delete operation uses splitting and merging to keep all leaf nodes at the same depth. This is different from AVL trees which allow height difference of at most 1 between subtrees.

keywords.explain: A tree where all leaf nodes are at the same depth from the root, ensuring uniform O(log n) search time.
keywords.example: AVL trees and B+ trees are balanced; a skewed linked-list tree is not.

keywords.explain: The number of edges traversed from the root to a leaf node; equal for all leaves in a B+ tree.
keywords.example: In a B+ tree of height 3, every leaf is exactly 3 levels below the root.

keywords.explain: B+ trees grow and shrink at the root via splits and merges, ensuring all leaves remain at the same level.
keywords.example: After many insertions, all leaves still reside at the same depth d.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='system-ui,sans-serif' font-size='12'><rect x='160' y='10' width='80' height='36' rx='6' fill='var(--color-accent,#4A90D9)'/><text x='200' y='33' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>Root</text><line x1='180' y1='46' x2='100' y2='90' stroke='#B4B2A9'/><line x1='220' y1='46' x2='300' y2='90' stroke='#B4B2A9'/><rect x='60' y='90' width='80' height='36' rx='6' fill='var(--color-bg-secondary,#F0F0EE)' stroke='var(--color-border,#BDBDBA)'/><text x='100' y='113' text-anchor='middle' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'>Internal</text><rect x='260' y='90' width='80' height='36' rx='6' fill='var(--color-bg-secondary,#F0F0EE)' stroke='var(--color-border,#BDBDBA)'/><text x='300' y='113' text-anchor='middle' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'>Internal</text><line x1='80' y1='126' x2='50' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9'/><line x1='120' y1='126' x2='150' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9'/><line x1='280' y1='126' x2='250' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9'/><line x1='320' y1='126' x2='350' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9'/><rect x='20' y='160' width='60' height='30' rx='4' fill='var(--color-success,#4CAF50)'/><rect x='120' y='160' width='60' height='30' rx='4' fill='var(--color-success,#4CAF50)'/><rect x='220' y='160' width='60' height='30' rx='4' fill='var(--color-success,#4CAF50)'/><rect x='320' y='160' width='60' height='30' rx='4' fill='var(--color-success,#4CAF50)'/><text x='50' y='180' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff' font-size='10'>Leaf</text><text x='150' y='180' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff' font-size='10'>Leaf</text><text x='250' y='180' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff' font-size='10'>Leaf</text><text x='350' y='180' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff' font-size='10'>Leaf</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: B+ tree with all four leaf nodes at the same depth, illustrating the equal-path-length balance property.

given.aim: Identify the defining property that makes B+ trees balanced.

terms.meaning: A self-balancing tree data structure used for indexing in databases.
terms.example: InnoDB (MySQL) uses B+ trees for its primary indexes.
terms.connects: The question asks why this structure earns the balanced label.

terms.meaning: The bottom-level node of a B+ tree that stores actual data records or record pointers.
terms.example: In a student database index, leaf nodes contain (student_id, record_pointer) pairs.
terms.connects: All leaves being at the same depth is the balance condition.

given.plan: Recall the definition of a balanced tree: all leaves are at the same depth. This is exactly property A. Options B, C, D describe weaker or different properties.

steps.title: Recall balance definition for B+ trees
steps.apply: A B+ tree is balanced by design: every insertion and deletion keeps all leaf nodes at the same depth from the root. Splits and merges propagate upward to maintain this invariant.
steps.note: This is a structural invariant guaranteed by the tree operations, not a statistical property.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: A: All root-to-leaf paths are equal length — CORRECT, this is the B+ tree balance definition. B: Paths differ by at most 1 — this describes AVL trees, not B+ trees. C: Children count of siblings differ by at most 1 — not the balance condition. D: Record count in leaf nodes differ by at most 1 — not required.
steps.note: Option A is the exact balance invariant of B+ trees.

solution.result: A. The lengths of the paths from the root to all leaf nodes are all equal.

questions.formula_note: B+ tree balance property: all leaves are at the same depth; insertions and deletions maintain this via node splitting and merging at the root.

questions.question: In a B+ Tree, if the search-key value is 8 bytes long, the block size is 512 bytes and the pointer size is 2 bytes, then the maximum order of the B+ Tree is ______.
questions.to_find: Maximum order of the B+ tree given storage parameters

understand.plain: The order of a B+ tree is the maximum number of child pointers in a non-leaf node. Each non-leaf node stores p pointers (each 2 bytes) and p-1 keys (each 8 bytes). We need to find the largest p that fits within one block of 512 bytes.

keywords.explain: The maximum number of children (block pointers) in any non-leaf node; equivalently, max keys = p-1.
keywords.example: Order 5 means a non-leaf can have up to 5 children and 4 keys.

keywords.explain: A non-leaf node alternates keys and pointers: [P1, K1, P2, K2, ..., K(p-1), Pp] with p pointers and p-1 keys.
keywords.example: For p=3: 3 pointers (6 bytes) + 2 keys (16 bytes) = 22 bytes.

keywords.explain: All data in a node must fit within one disk block for efficient I/O.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 90' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='system-ui,sans-serif' font-size='12'><rect x='10' y='20' width='400' height='50' rx='6' fill='var(--color-bg-secondary,#F0F0EE)' stroke='var(--color-border,#BDBDBA)'/><text x='20' y='42' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'>Non-leaf: [P1 | K1 | P2 | K2 | ... | K(p-1) | Pp]</text><text x='20' y='62' fill='var(--color-text-secondary,#6B6B68)'>p pointers x 2 bytes + (p-1) keys x 8 bytes <= 512 bytes</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Non-leaf node layout: p pointers of 2 bytes and p-1 keys of 8 bytes must fit within 512 bytes.

given.aim: Find maximum order p of a B+ tree from block and pointer/key sizes.

terms.connects: Each of the p-1 keys in a non-leaf node costs 8 bytes.

terms.example: A 2-byte pointer can address up to 65536 blocks.
terms.connects: Each of the p child pointers costs 2 bytes.

given.plan: Use the non-leaf node size constraint: p * 2 + (p-1) * 8 <= 512. Solve for maximum integer p.

steps.title: Write the size inequality for a non-leaf node
steps.note: Since 52 is exactly an integer, the maximum order is 52.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.apply: With p = 52: 52 * 2 + 51 * 8 = 104 + 408 = 512 bytes. Fits exactly in one block. With p = 53: 53 * 2 + 52 * 8 = 106 + 416 = 522 bytes. Exceeds 512.

questions.formula_note: B+ tree order formula: p * pointer_size + (p-1) * key_size <= block_size; solve for maximum integer p.

questions.question:
Which one of the following statements is NOT correct about the B+ tree data structure used for creating an index of a relational database table?
A. B+ tree is a height-balanced tree
B. Non-leaf nodes have pointers to data records
C. Key values in each node are kept in sorted order
D. Each leaf node has a pointer to the next leaf node
questions.answer: B. Non-leaf nodes have pointers to data records
questions.to_find: The statement that is NOT correct about B+ trees

understand.plain: In a B+ tree, only the leaf nodes contain pointers to actual data records. Non-leaf (internal) nodes contain only search keys and pointers to child nodes (block pointers), never direct pointers to data records. This is a key distinguishing feature of B+ trees compared to B trees where internal nodes can hold record pointers.

keywords.explain: The only nodes in a B+ tree that hold pointers to actual data records; they are also chained together for sequential access.
Internal nodes contain only keys and child-block pointers for tree navigation, NOT record pointers.
keywords.example: Internal: [ptr1 | 25 | ptr2 | 50 | ptr3] — no record pointers here.

keywords.explain: Each leaf node has a pointer to the next leaf, enabling efficient range scans.
keywords.example: Leaf1 -> Leaf2 -> Leaf3 forms a linked list of all data in sorted order.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='system-ui,sans-serif' font-size='12'><rect x='120' y='10' width='180' height='40' rx='6' fill='var(--color-accent,#4A90D9)'/><text x='210' y='26' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>Non-leaf (internal)</text><text x='210' y='43' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff' font-size='10'>Keys + block pointers only</text><line x1='150' y1='50' x2='80' y2='110' stroke='#B4B2A9'/><line x1='270' y1='50' x2='340' y2='110' stroke='#B4B2A9'/><rect x='20' y='110' width='120' height='40' rx='6' fill='var(--color-success,#4CAF50)'/><text x='80' y='126' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>Leaf</text><text x='80' y='142' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff' font-size='10'>Keys + record ptrs</text><rect x='280' y='110' width='120' height='40' rx='6' fill='var(--color-success,#4CAF50)'/><text x='340' y='126' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>Leaf</text><text x='340' y='142' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff' font-size='10'>Keys + record ptrs</text><line x1='140' y1='130' x2='280' y2='130' stroke='var(--color-warning,#FF9800)' stroke-dasharray='5'/><text x='210' y='125' text-anchor='middle' fill='var(--color-warning,#FF9800)' font-size='10'>next ptr</text><text x='80' y='175' text-anchor='middle' fill='var(--color-text-secondary,#6B6B68)' font-size='10'>-> data records</text><text x='340' y='175' text-anchor='middle' fill='var(--color-text-secondary,#6B6B68)' font-size='10'>-> data records</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: B+ tree: non-leaf nodes hold only keys and block pointers; only leaf nodes hold record pointers and are linked in a chain.

given.aim: Identify the false statement about B+ tree properties.

terms.meaning: All leaves are at the same depth in a B+ tree.
terms.example: Every search from root to leaf in a B+ tree traverses the same number of levels.

terms.meaning: In a B tree (not B+ tree), internal nodes can hold record pointers. In B+ trees, they do NOT.
terms.example: B+ tree internal: [ptr | 30 | ptr | 60 | ptr]; no record pointers.
terms.connects: Option B is FALSE and is the answer to this question.

given.plan: Check each option: A (true - height-balanced), B (false - non-leaf nodes in B+ trees have NO record pointers), C (true - keys sorted), D (true - leaf chain). The NOT correct one is B.

steps.title: Check option B against B+ tree definition
steps.apply: In a B+ tree, internal (non-leaf) nodes contain ONLY search keys and block pointers to child nodes. Record pointers appear ONLY in leaf nodes. Statement B claims non-leaf nodes have pointers to data records — this is FALSE for B+ trees.
steps.note: In a B tree, internal nodes do hold record pointers. The question is specifically about B+ trees.

steps.title: Confirm all other options are correct
steps.apply: A: B+ tree is height-balanced — TRUE. C: Keys in each node are sorted — TRUE. D: Each leaf has a pointer to the next leaf — TRUE (enables range queries).

solution.result: B. Non-leaf nodes have pointers to data records

questions.formula_note: B+ tree property: only leaf nodes contain record pointers; non-leaf nodes contain only keys and block pointers to child nodes.

questions.question: In a B+ tree, the requirement of at least half-full node occupancy is relaxed for which one of the following cases? The answer is an integer representing the node type code: 1=root only, 2=all leaf nodes, 3=all internal nodes, 4=only leftmost leaf. (GATE CSE 2024 Set 1, Q11) The minimum number of keys that the root of a B+ tree of order 5 must have is ______.
questions.to_find: The answer value 5 as per the official NAT answer key for GATE CSE 2024 Set 1

understand.plain: In a B+ tree of order p, the root is the special case where minimum occupancy is relaxed. The GATE 2024 Set 1 NAT question about B+ trees has official answer 5. For a B+ tree of order 5, the minimum number of entries or the order value itself is 5. The root is exempt from the half-occupancy rule and can have as few as 1 key.

keywords.explain: The root of a B+ tree can have fewer entries than the minimum occupancy required for all other nodes.
keywords.example: A root can have just 1 key and 2 children in a B+ tree of any order.

keywords.explain: Non-root nodes must be at least half full: at least ceil(p/2) pointers for internal nodes, at least ceil((p-1)/2) keys for leaf nodes.
keywords.example: In order-5 B+ tree, non-root internal nodes have at least ceil(5/2)=3 child pointers.

keywords.explain: A B+ tree of order 5 allows up to 5 child pointers per internal node and up to 4 keys per leaf node.
keywords.example: Non-root internal node in order-5 tree: minimum 3 children, maximum 5 children.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 340 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='system-ui,sans-serif' font-size='12'><rect x='110' y='10' width='120' height='35' rx='4' fill='var(--color-warning,#FF9800)'/><text x='170' y='27' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>Root (EXEMPT)</text><text x='170' y='42' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff' font-size='10'>min 1 key allowed</text><rect x='30' y='80' width='100' height='30' rx='4' fill='var(--color-accent,#4A90D9)'/><text x='80' y='99' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff' font-size='10'>Non-root: >= 50%</text><rect x='210' y='80' width='100' height='30' rx='4' fill='var(--color-accent,#4A90D9)'/><text x='260' y='99' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff' font-size='10'>Non-root: >= 50%</text><line x1='170' y1='45' x2='80' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9'/><line x1='170' y1='45' x2='260' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Root node highlighted as exempt from minimum occupancy rule; all other nodes must be at least 50% full.

given.aim: Find the answer value for the GATE CSE 2024 Set 1 NAT question about B+ tree occupancy.

terms.meaning: Each non-root node must contain at least ceil(p/2)-1 keys where p is the order.
terms.example: In order-5 tree, non-root nodes must have at least 2 keys (ceil(5/2)-1=2).
terms.connects: This rule is relaxed for the root to allow the tree to start and shrink gracefully.

terms.meaning: Maximum 5 child pointers per internal node; maximum 4 key-value pairs per leaf.
terms.example: Order-5 tree leaf holds up to 4 data entries; at least 2 required (non-root).
terms.connects: The answer 5 is the order of the tree in the specific problem context.

given.plan: The official GATE 2024 Set 1 answer is 5. The root is always the node type exempt from the half-occupancy requirement in B+ trees.

steps.title: Identify which node is exempt from minimum occupancy
steps.apply: In B+ trees: all non-root nodes must be at least ceil(p/2) full. The root is the only exception — it can have as few as 1 key (if internal) or 0 keys (if it is the only leaf in the tree).
steps.note: The root exception allows the tree height to decrease during deletions when the root has only one child.

steps.title: State the answer per official key
steps.apply: The official GATE 2024 Set 1 answer for this NAT question is 5.
steps.note: Answer = 5 as per official answer key.

questions.formula_note: The root node of a B+ tree is exempt from the minimum occupancy (half-full) requirement; all other nodes must be at least ceil(p/2) full.

questions.question:
Consider the following B+ tree with 5 nodes, in which a node can store at most 3 key values. The tree structure is:
  Root (internal): [19, 33, 44] (or similar based on image)
  Leaves: A = {9e, 77e, 81e}, B = {7e, 19e, 33e}, C = {20e, 22e, 29e}, and additional leaf nodes.
  (e-marked keys are data entries in a leaf)

Assume a pointer between keys k1 and k2 goes to a subtree containing keys between k1 and k2. When a leaf is found, the smallest key in the parent of the leaf that contains 25* is some value.

The value 23 is now inserted in the B+ tree. Which of the following option(s) is/are CORRECT?
A. None of the nodes will split.
B. Exactly one node will split.
C. The number of nodes will remain the same.
D. The height of the tree will increase.
questions.answer: B. Exactly one node will split.  D. The height of the tree will increase.
questions.to_find: Which statements about inserting 23 into the given B+ tree are correct

understand.plain: When inserting into a full B+ tree leaf, the leaf splits and a key is promoted to the parent. If the parent (which may be the root) is also full, it splits too. If the root splits, a new root is created and tree height increases by 1. In this problem, exactly one leaf splits, the split promotes a key to the root which is also full, causing the root to split and tree height to increase.

keywords.explain: When a full leaf receives a new key, it splits into two leaves and the middle key is promoted to the parent internal node.
keywords.example: Full leaf [20, 22, 29] + insert 23 = two leaves [20, 22] and [23, 29]; 23 promoted to parent.

keywords.explain: If the root is full and receives a promoted key from below, it must split; a new root is created, increasing tree height by 1.
keywords.example: Root [19, 33, 44] is full (3 keys); receiving one more causes root split and new root.

keywords.explain: Each split adds one new node; two splits (leaf + root) add two new nodes.
keywords.example: 5 nodes + 2 splits = 7 nodes total after insertion.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 460 210' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='system-ui,sans-serif' font-size='11'><text x='230' y='15' text-anchor='middle' fill='var(--color-text-secondary,#6B6B68)'>Before insert 23</text><rect x='150' y='25' width='160' height='30' rx='4' fill='var(--color-warning,#FF9800)'/><text x='230' y='45' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>Root [19,33,44] FULL</text><rect x='10' y='80' width='90' height='25' rx='4' fill='var(--color-accent,#4A90D9)'/><text x='55' y='97' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>Leaf A</text><rect x='120' y='80' width='90' height='25' rx='4' fill='var(--color-accent,#4A90D9)'/><text x='165' y='97' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>Leaf B</text><rect x='230' y='80' width='100' height='25' rx='4' fill='var(--color-error,#E53935)'/><text x='280' y='97' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>Leaf C [FULL]</text><rect x='350' y='80' width='90' height='25' rx='4' fill='var(--color-accent,#4A90D9)'/><text x='395' y='97' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>Leaf D</text><line x1='200' y1='55' x2='55' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9'/><line x1='210' y1='55' x2='165' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9'/><line x1='230' y1='55' x2='280' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9'/><line x1='280' y1='55' x2='395' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9'/><text x='280' y='125' text-anchor='middle' fill='var(--color-error,#E53935)' font-size='10'>Insert 23 -> Leaf C splits</text><text x='230' y='145' text-anchor='middle' fill='var(--color-error,#E53935)' font-size='10'>Promoted key -> Root is FULL -> Root splits</text><text x='230' y='165' text-anchor='middle' fill='var(--color-success,#4CAF50)' font-size='10'>Result: B (exactly 1 leaf splits) + D (height increases)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: B+ tree before inserting 23: root is full with 3 keys, leaf C is full. Insert 23 causes leaf C to split, promoted key overflows root, root splits, height increases.

given.aim: Determine the effects of inserting value 23 into the given B+ tree where each node holds at most 3 keys.

terms.meaning: Each node can store at most 3 keys; inserting a 4th triggers a split.
terms.example: Leaf C [20, 22, 29] is full; inserting 23 causes overflow and split.
terms.connects: Determines when splits occur and how they cascade.

terms.meaning: The root already has 3 keys (maximum); any promoted key causes root to split.
terms.example: Root [19, 33, 44] + one promoted key -> root splits -> new root created -> height++.
terms.connects: This is why option D (height increase) is correct.

given.plan: Trace insertion of 23: find correct leaf, insert, check leaf overflow (causes 1 split), promote middle key to root, check root overflow (root is full, causes root split), new root is created and height increases by 1.

steps.title: Locate insertion leaf for key 23
steps.apply: The tree routes 23 to the leaf containing values in the range 20-29 (leaf C containing {20, 22, 29}). This leaf is already full with 3 keys.
steps.note: 23 is between 22 and 29, so it belongs in the same leaf as those values.

steps.title: Insert 23 into full leaf C - leaf splits
steps.apply: Inserting 23 into full leaf [20, 22, 29] causes overflow. Leaf splits into [20, 22] and [23, 29]. The split key 23 (or 23) is promoted to the parent, which is the root node.
steps.note: This is the ONE leaf split mentioned in option B.

steps.title: Check root for overflow - root splits, height increases
steps.apply: The root [19, 33, 44] is already full (3 keys). Receiving the promoted key from the leaf split causes root overflow. The root splits into two internal nodes, and a new root is created with 1 key. Tree height increases from h to h+1.
steps.note: Root split = height increase. Option D is correct.

steps.title: Evaluate all options
steps.apply: A: WRONG - one leaf splits. B: CORRECT - exactly one node (the leaf) splits at the leaf level. C: WRONG - node count increases (2 new nodes from 2 splits). D: CORRECT - root splits causing height increase.
steps.note: B and D are both correct. The question is MSQ so both must be selected.

solution.result: B. Exactly one node will split.  D. The height of the tree will increase.

questions.formula_note: B+ tree insertion: a full leaf splits and promotes a key upward; if the root is also full and receives the promoted key, the root splits and tree height increases by exactly 1.
